original_abstract: While iron represents an ideal, earth-abundant alternative to noble metals in photoactive complexes, developing emissive and photochemically functional iron compounds remains a formidable challenge due to the rapid deactivation of their charge-transfer states. Consequently, emissive iron complexes are exceedingly rare, and dual emission has remained entirely elusive until now. In this work, we present the Fe(III) complex [Fe(ImP)₂][PF₆] (where HImP = 1,1′-(1,3-phenylene)bis(3-methyl-1-imidazol-2-ylidene)), which exhibits unprecedented Janus-type dual emission originating from both ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) and metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) states. This unique photophysical profile is enabled by a ligand framework that integrates four N-heterocyclic carbenes with two cyclometalating aryl units. The low-lying π* orbitals of the cyclometalating architecture stabilize the MLCT states, preventing their relaxation into LMCT states. With a substantial lifetime of 4.6 ns, the highly potent MLCT-dominated state can drive both reductive and oxidative electron transfer reactions. These findings provide a new blueprint for light-harvesting iron complexes and establish a foundation for the use of iron in advanced photoredox catalysis.
humanized_abstract: Although iron is an attractive, nonprecious metal that is abundant in earth crust, emissive and photochemically active iron complexes are still hard to prepare as the charge-transfer states are deactivated very quickly, giving only few emissive iron complexes, none of which have been reported to show dual emission. We herein report a Fe(III) complex [Fe(ImP)₂][PF₆] where HImP is 1,1′-(1,3-phenylene)bis(3-methyl-1-imidazol-2-ylidene) which shows Janus-type dual emission behavior from ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) and metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) states. This unique photophysical behavior is achieved using a new class of ligands that contain four N-heterocyclic carbenes and two cyclometalating aryl rings. The presence of low lying π* orbitals of the cyclometalating units maintains the MLCT states rather than allowing them to be deactivated to LMCT states; a state which in this complex has a large lifetime of 4.6 ns and which is very reactive for reducing as well as for oxidizing the substrates. Our work provides a new model for iron-based photosensitizers and opens new perspectives in the field of photoredox catalysis using iron.

This unique photophysical behavior is achieved using a new class of ligands that contain four N-heterocyclic carbenes and two cyclometalating aryl rings. The presence of low lying π* orbitals of the cyclometalating units maintains the MLCT states rather than allowing them to be deactivated to LMCT states; a state which in this complex has a large lifetime of 4.6 ns and which is very reactive for reducing as well as for oxidizing the substrates. Our work provides a new model for iron-based photosensitizers and opens new perspectives in the field of photoredox catalysis using iron.
